introduction: An inflammatory skin condition involving the skin and/or mucus membranes. It is a chronic, pruritic and papular condition. Some of the drugs known to cause lichen planus (LP): Chloroquine, Quinacrine, Quinidine, Gold, Streptomycin, Tetracycline, NSAIDs, Phenothiazines, Hydrochlorothiazide.

features: LP has been found in children, young and middle-aged adults
Skin lesions are flat-topped polygonal papules with a characteristic colour
Violaceous in fair skinned people, but slate-grey on black skin
Like psoriasis, lesions often occur on sites of trauma and scratch marks (Koebner's or isomorphic phenomenon)
Wickham's striae are fine white streaks present on the tops of papules
The lesions are distributed mainly on: Flexor surfaces of the wrist, Lumbar area, The penis, tongue, buccal and vaginal mucous membranes
On the buccal mucous membrane, it may present as white reticulate (lacy) pattern or plaque which may after several years transgress into squamous cell carcinoma
The nails are also affected with: Pitting, roughening and splitting (trachyonychia); Thickening (pachyonychia); Encroachment of the nail fold on the nail plate (pterygium unguium)
Total destruction of all 20 nails may precede, accompany, or follow the onset of skin lesions
The hair follicles in the scalp may also be affected (lichen planopilaris) with post-inflammatory scarring alopecia
Hepatitis C infection is found with greater frequency in lichen planus than in controls
Healing of the skin lesions leaves post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation

drug: Topical corticosteroids: Beclomethasone dipropionate 0.1% cream; apply 1 - 2 times daily
Bethamethasone valerate 0.1% cream and ointment; apply 1 - 2 times daily
For isolated or hyperkeratotic lesions apply corticosteroids under occlusion or use intralesional triamcinolone
Scalp lesions: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% lotion; apply thinly 1 - 2 times daily for up to 4 weeks
Mouth lesions: Triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% in adhesive base apply a thin layer 2 - 4 times daily for a maximum of 5 days
Tretinoin 0.025% cream; apply thinly 1 - 2 times daily
Systemic corticosteroids: Prednisolone 20 - 40 mg orally daily for several weeks
Triamcinolone acetonide 40 mg intramuscularly once or twice at a 6-week interval
Ciclosporin 2.5 mg/kg daily in two divided doses; increase to maximum 5 mg/kg daily if needed

differential_diagnoses: Oral lesions: Erosive lesions may mimic Aphthous stomatitis and herpes simplex
White plaques may be confused with pre-malignant leukoplakia
complications: Rarely, squamous cell carcinoma of oral and hypertrophic lichen planus
